Summaries

five sample summaries

[1]                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
[2] In this TED talk, Angelica Dass talksabout skin color, identity,and racism. She explains that society often describes people using simple categories such as “black” and “white”, even though human skin colors are much more diverse. She shares stories about her family and how their different skin tones influenced her way of thinking. To challenge stereotypes and discrimination, she created a photography project called Humanae. In this project, she photographs people from different countries and backgrounds and matches their skin tones with Pantone colors. More than 3000 people have participated in the project. Her work shows that there are countless skin colors and that humans cannot be separated into only a few racial groupes.She also explains that her project is used in education and research to help people think differently about race and diversity. Through her talk, Dass encourages people to respect differences and understand that all humans are equal regardless of skin color.
[3]                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
[4]                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
[5] Angelica grew up in a family of people of various skin colors. Her grandparents had a skin color close to white, and her father had a skin color close to black. She herself has dark tones of skin. She was shocked that she didn't have the right skin tone in her painting class. Just because her skin color was dark, she was discriminated against at various moments in her life. Even in modern times when slavery has been abolished, the tendency to judge people by their skin color continues. She started a project called Humanae after marrying a Spanish. More than 3,000 people from 13 countries participated in the project, which shows their skin tone. Not only does her project serve as a resource for people studying art and various researchers, but it is also used by teachers in schools that allow children to recognize their skin tone. Discrimination does not disappear naturally. People's continuous efforts are needed in the future.                                                      
3 Levels:  ...

word count for summary


Opinions

five sample opinions

[1]                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        
[2]                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        
[3] I think this talk was very interesting and important. Before watching it, I did not think deeply about how people are judged by their skin color. She showed that there are many different skin colors, and no one can be simply colors like black or white. I was surprised that the skin color can be divided so finely. I liked her project of using real color codes because it makes people think differently about race and people can think of their own identity from their skin color. I was also interested in her project because it included people from many countries and backgrounds. Especially in Japan, compared to other countries, it is rare to feel the diversity of skin colors in everyday life. That's why this project is more interesting. Her talk teaches us that everyone is unique and should respect each other. And this talk helped me understand diversity better and made me realize that respecting differences more is important.
[4] Before I went to Canada as an exchange student, I never thought deeply about skin colors. However, my host family there had completely different skin tones as well. This background made me strongly agree with her project in the video. Labeling people as "Black," "white," or "yellow" is very easy, but actual human skin color is much more diverse and unique. Her work using Pantone codes clearly proves that everyone has their own beautiful shade, which science also supports today. I think her project is wonderful because it teaches children in schools to look at individual colors rather than traditional racial categories to reduce discrimination in the world.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               
[5]                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        
3 Levels:  ...
